entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2601
entry.content:
January’s release of SAP Asset Performance Management introduces updates across industrial systems, reliability assessments, asset health monitoring, and performance analysis. Highlights include bulk activation and deactivation of system elements, more flexible risk and criticality assessment outcomes, CAP-based reliability assessment applications with extensibility, configurable RCM consequence questionnaires, validity periods for assessments, time-window–based rule processing, and embedded analytics dashboards.

Before we start here's a webcast tip!  Do join us for the upcoming What's New webcast on February 18, 2026 where we'll demo the highlights from the last few months. Sign up here: What’s New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2602.

Let's take a closer look at what’s new by business capability.

  • Asset Information and Data Management
  • Asset Risk Assessment
  • Asset Reliability Engineering
  • Asset Health Monitoring
  • Asset Performance Analysis

Asset Information and Data Management

Industrial Systems: From Manual Clicks to Scalable System Modeling

The challenge

System elements could be activated or deactivated, but only one at a time. There was no filtering or sorting to easily identify which elements were active or inactive. Excel uploads didn’t support activation or deactivation at all. For large system hierarchies, this made modeling slow, manual, and error-prone.

We wanted to provide reliability engineers with a faster, more scalable way to manage complex industrial systems — especially in environments with hundreds or thousands of system elements.

What’s new

Users can now:

  • Select multiple system elements and activate or deactivate them in one action
  • Filter and sort elements by activation status and other criteria
  • Use Excel uploads with a new Activate Element column to perform bulk updates

The outcome

  • Significantly reduced time and effort for system maintenance
  • Bulk operations that scale with system complexity
  • Improved usability for day-to-day modeling work

To read the full list of new features see here.
For more information,  see the SAP Help Portal:
  • Manage Industrial Systems
  • Activate or Deactivate a System Element
  • Export or Import a System Structure
  • Creating an Industrial System from Excel

Asset Risk Assessment

Risk & Criticality Assessments: From Forced Choices to Meaningful Classification

The challenge

Risk and Criticality Assessment Templates only supported a fixed set of resultant actions such as FMEA, RCM, OEM Guidelines, PM Review, and Run to Fail. In practice, not every assessment outcome fits neatly into one of these categories — leading to forced or misleading classifications.

Reliability engineers needed a way to document outcomes accurately, even when predefined actions didn’t apply.

What’s new

Templates now include two additional actions: “Others” and “Not Applicable.” This allows teams to capture generic or exceptional outcomes without misclassifying results.

New Actions ' Others' and 'Not Applicable' in app 'Manage Templates for Risk and Criticality Assessments''

The outcome:

  • More accurate and meaningful assessment documentation
  • Better decision-making by avoiding misclassification
  • Increased usability in varied operational contexts

Asset Reliability Engineering

Reliability Assessments: From Rigid Apps to an Extensible SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) Foundation

The challenge

Both the RCM Assessments and Strategy Assessments for Classes applications were built on a foundation that didn’t support field extensibility, timeline changes, or standard search and sort capabilities across list and object detail views. This made it difficult to tailor assessments to industry-specific needs, scale them across different asset classes, and deliver a consistent, modern user experience.

In practice, reliability teams were constrained by fixed data structures and limited UI flexibility — even though real-world reliability engineering demands customization, evolution, and long-term scalability.

Customers needed a future-ready foundation that supports customization, consistency, and continuous evolution — without forcing separate design patterns for different types of reliability assessments.

What’s new

SAP introduces new further CAP-based applications for both RCM Assessments and Strategy Assessments for Classes, built on the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) and OData APIs.

New entry points to new 'Manage RCM Assessments' and 'Manage Strategy Assessment for Classes' apps based on the SAP Cloud Application Programming framework

These new applications provide:

  • Out-of-the-box extensibility for adding custom fields. For more information, see the Extensibility Guide on the SAP Help Portal.
  • A more intuitive, consistent, and efficient user experience
  • A centralized foundation for industry-specific data capture and customizations

Both applications are replacing their current versions after a transition period.

The outcome:

  • Greater flexibility to tailor assessments to asset classes and industries
  • Improved usability and consistency across reliability workflows
  • A scalable, future-proof architecture for ongoing innovation

For more information on release timelines, implementation, support, and known limitations, refer to SAP Note 3603407.

Read also this blog by Archi Garg on extensibility Extending SAP Asset Performance Management: Three Approaches to Tailoring Your Asset Operations

 

RCM Consequence Evaluation: New Custom Questionnaires

The challenge

Until now, the Manage RCM Assessments application supported consequence evaluation using a default questionnaire. While this provided a standardized starting point, it didn’t reflect the reality that organizations operate in different industries, regulatory environments, and risk contexts. A generic questionnaire often limited the relevance and effectiveness of RCM assessments.

Customers needed a way to align consequence evaluation with their own operational and risk management strategies — without being constrained by a fixed structure.

What’s new

Users can now configure the RCM consequence evaluation questionnaire to match organizational needs. Administrators and reliability engineers can define their own evaluation framework for technical objects, making RCM assessments more context-aware and meaningful.

How to configure an RCM consequence evaluation in the Configuration Manager and the custom questionnaire in the 'Manage RCM Assessments' app

The outcome

  • Greater process flexibility
  • Industry- and context-specific consequence evaluations
  • More effective and relevant RCM results

 To learn how to create a custom questionnaire read the SAP Help Portal Custom Questionnaires.

Assessment Validity Periods: From Static Analyses to Living Assessments

The challenge

There was no way to define a validity period for RCM assessments and Strategy Assessments for Classes, therefore there was no built-in mechanism to track when they should be revisited or to align recommendations with assessment relevance.

Customers needed a way to ensure assessments stay current and to systematically trigger reviews at the right time.

What’s new

Users can now assign start and end dates to assessments. Validity periods can be propagated to linked recommendations, and automatic tracking and notifications alert teams when an assessment is due for review.

New validity period fields in the 'Manage Strategy Assessments for Classes' app

The outcome

  • Clear definition and tracking of assessment relevance
  • Improved visibility through date-based filtering
  • Consistent recommendations aligned with assessment validity

Asset Health Monitoring

Rule Processing: From Static Aggregates to Time-Aware Decisions

The challenge

Condition-based rules supported aggregates like last, first, and previous, but without any time-window context. This meant rules couldn’t evaluate data within a bounded time range, limiting both rule definition and the accuracy of decisions based on historical values.

Customers needed finer control over how rules interpret data — especially in scenarios where recent trends matters more than long-term values.

What’s new

Aggregate functions now support optional time windows. Rules can evaluate last, first, and previous values within a defined period, such as the last hour or day. This ensures rule evaluations are based on the most relevant data and enables more accurate, context-aware decisions.

An example rule showing how temperature conditions can be evaluated using window-based aggregate values to trigger a high-temperature alert

The outcome:

  • More accurate and context-aware decisions
  • Improved operational efficiency
  • Greater flexibility in rule design

For more information, see the SAP Help Portal Working with Aggregates.

 

Asset Performance Analysis

Embedded Analytics in APM: Solving Reliability Problems Faster

Embedded analytics dashboards—including the option to link external SAP Analytics Cloud dashboards—are now available for embedded IoT tenants.
For reliability engineers, this means answers faster, in context, and without jumping between tools.

As a reliability engineer, I need to:

  • Understand which assets are driving risk
  • Identify patterns behind failures and performance degradation
  • Validate whether corrective actions are actually working

The challenge is rarely a lack of data—it’s connecting the right data to the right asset at the right moment.

See asset data together

Embedded analytics dashboards provide interactive visualizations across technical objects in SAP Asset Performance Management.
They combine multiple assessment types into meaningful dashboards, exposing key indicators alongside risks, liabilities, and alerts—all within the asset management context, not in a separate reporting tool.

Working with the data

Configurable charts and tables based on analytical data objects support analysis at both summary and detail level.
This allows comparison of similar assets, identifying recurring failure patterns, and prioritizing issues based on impact, not volume.

Creating dashboards

Dashboards can be created and adapted directly in APM using embedded SAP Analytics Cloud.
As investigations evolve, I can adjust KPIs, filters, and visualizations without leaving APM or waiting for reports, supporting guided exploration of data from overview to detail.

Connecting to external SAC dashboards

When broader or cross-domain analysis is required, external SAP Analytics Cloud dashboards can be linked to embedded analytics dashboards in APM. 
For this, a separate SAP Analytics Cloud license is required.

For more information, see the SAP Help Portal Manage Analytics Dashboards.

 

Stay Informed and Get Involved

  • Don't forget to sign up for the upcoming webcast on February 18, 2026  What’s New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2602
  • You can also explore our latest blog posts here, including topics such as Embedded IoT by @florian_weigold, Industrial Systems by @SonamKinger, and the extensibility blog by @archigarg mentioned earlier.
  • Find out what's up and coming in the SAP Road Map Explorer.
  • For an exhaustive list of all new features, visit the SAP Help Portal - What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management.. 
  • Send us your improvement requests. Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session.
  • Check the January update of the APM link collection.

entry.title: SAP Business AI & Sustainability: WHAT
entry.content:
SAP is leading the way in enterprise management by integrating sustainability into its core business suite, powered by applications, data, and AI. SAP Business Suite isn’t just a collection of tools—it’s a transformation engine that drives continuous innovation. By embedding sustainability into business processes, SAP helps organizations manage risk, achieve compliance, and unlock new value while meeting regulatory and environmental goals.

SAP Drives Sustainability with Applications, Data, and AI

  1. Applications: Embedded Sustainability SAP’s end-to-end business suite ensures that every transaction is part of a unified framework. This standardization allows sustainability data to flow seamlessly across processes like procure-to-pay and record-to-report. By embedding sustainability into core applications, businesses can track, report, and improve sustainability metrics efficiently.
  2. Data: A Single Source of Truth Sustainability data is often fragmented across different systems. SAP’s Business Data Cloud harmonizes this data into one unified model, ensuring consistency and accuracy. This single source of truth enables better reporting, deeper insights, and confident decision-making across all sustainability initiatives.
  3. AI: Intelligent Automation and Insights SAP Business AI goes beyond basic automation by using AI agents to optimize processes in real time. These agents help reduce errors, lower costs, and improve efficiency while ensuring sustainability-aware decisions. AI also enhances predictive insights, helping businesses balance profitability with environmental impact.

Key Sustainability Focus Areas

SAP supports sustainability across four critical areas:

  • Sustainability Management – Helps set strategies, track ESG metrics, and ensure compliance with global standards.
  • Carbon Accounting – Measures and manages emissions while integrating with financial accounting.
  • Sustainable Supply Chain – Ensures transparency, circularity, and ethical sourcing across the value chain.
  • Operational Compliance – Maintains adherence to environmental, safety, and ESG regulations.

Why SAP’s Approach Stands Out

Unlike siloed sustainability solutions, SAP integrates sustainability into the core of business operations. This ensures that sustainability isn’t an afterthought but a strategic driver of growth. By leveraging AI, businesses can automate complex sustainability processes, gain actionable insights, and make data-driven decisions that reduce risk and unlock new opportunities.

entry.title: Odata V4 Virus Scan Issue
entry.content:
Hi SAP,

 

We are seeing an issue in Odata V4 calls to the backend system PSD, the error message we get is Virus scan profile /IWBEP/V4/ODATA_UPLOAD is not active 

We checked below SAP Notes which mentions to activate the profile in VSCANPROFILE transaction.

https://me.sap.com/notes/3634529/E

https://me.sap.com/notes/3027559

However what we want to understand is

  1. What impact it has on the ODATA V4 calls incoming to the system if we select SCANALLFILES scan configuration parameter while activating this profile ?
  2. What is the performance, usage addition on the system overall with having this profile activated on the system, as there may be countless such incomings calls throughout the business day? Considering if we select SCANALLFILES scan configuration parameter while activating this profile ?
  3. Is there any specific settings, recommendations from SAP ?

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Odata V4 Virus Scan Issue
entry.content:
Hello,

Activating the virus scan profile /IWBEP/V4/ODATA_UPLOAD with the SCANALLFILES option ensures that all OData V4 upload payloads are virus-checked before processing. While this introduces some additional processing overhead for upload requests, it does not affect read-only OData calls and is the SAP-recommended and security-compliant configuration. Any performance impact depends mainly on upload volume, file sizes, and antivirus engine capacity.

Notice that SAP's general guidance is to:

- Activate /IWBEP/V4/ODATA_UPLOAD

- Use SCANALLFILES for productive systems

- Ensure a properly sized and supported virus scan provider is configured

Nevertheless, here are some additional best practices:

- Use a high-performance, SAP-certified antivirus engine

- Monitor scan-related performance using below backend t-codes:

ST22 (dumps)

SM50 / SM66 (work processes)

If performance issues occur: check the antivirus infrastructure or review upload payload sizes and usage patterns.

entry.title: Unlocking SAP Treasury - TRM - FAQ - Treasury - SAP Treasury Operations
entry.content:
Unlocking the Value of SAP TRM for Corporate Treasury

SAP Treasury and Risk Management (TRM) supports the complete end‑to‑end lifecycle of financial transactions between a corporation and its banking partners. This includes the full processing of instruments such as loans, deposits, FX deals, derivatives, securities, and more. For each transaction, the system automatically generates detailed cashflow schedules and posts all related accounting entries, including principal movements, interest accruals, repayments, fees, and bank charges.

Month‑end activities are fully integrated, covering current‑portion reclassification, amortization, accrual/deferral, foreign‑currency valuation, and mark‑to‑market or fair‑value measurement in accordance with IFRS and local GAAP requirements.

TRM includes more than 120 contract types, reflecting the breadth of financial instruments used by corporate treasuries worldwide. These processes fall under Transaction Management and are complemented by powerful analytical components such as Credit Risk Analyzer, Market Risk Analyzer, Portfolio Analyzer, and Market Data Management. Together, they provide comprehensive exposure monitoring, valuation, risk assessment, and performance analysis across the entire treasury portfolio. Master Data Tricks

1.    Treasury and Risk management vs Corporate Treasury requirement

There is a common misconception that purchasing a license for SAP Treasury and Risk Management (TRM) will cover the entire scope of a company’s treasury operations. In reality, SAP treasury functionality is distributed across multiple modules, and TRM alone does not fulfill all requirements.

Treasury and Risk Management (TRM): Financial instruments, risk management, valuations, and accounting.

Cash and Liquidity Management (CLM): Cash position, liquidity forecasting, and bank account management.

Payment and Bank Communication (BCM): Payment processing, approvals, and bank connectivity.

A complete treasury solution requires all three modules working together. The link below provides additional detail on this topic.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/4785100c7f20412f95174592eff35939/7bc4a1572cfbb576e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

2.    SAP best Practice – Learn TRM Processes

SAP Best Practices are the most effective starting point for learning TRM processes, especially for new users. They provide complete, sequential process flows for all commonly used Treasury functions. Every user and junior consultant who wants to build a strong foundation in the TRM module should begin by exploring the SAP Best Practice scenarios, as they offer structured guidance, standard process steps, and end‑to‑end examples aligned with SAP’s recommended approach.

 

 

3.    Monthly/Periodic Closing – Valuation posting

From an accounting perspective, Treasury operations require multiple financial postings that impact both the Balance Sheet and the Profit & Loss statement. These include interest accruals, FX valuations, bond amortization, current‑portion reclassification, and other postings depending on the organization’s requirements and the contract types in use.

In SAP TRM, all relevant data is captured at the time of deal creation. Based on this information, the system generates structural flows, which serve as the foundation for all valuation and closing activities. Month‑end and periodic closing postings derive their values directly from these structural flows. To execute these processes, transactions such as TPM44, TPM1, TPM18, and others are run as part of the closing cycle. Common valuation and closing processes include:

- Amortization (linear or effective interest method): Posts the positive or negative difference between the purchase value and the face value over the life of the instrument.

- Price Valuation (Mark‑to‑Market): Adjusts the book value based on current market prices, resulting in unrealized gains or losses.

- Security Valuation: Based on yield curves and clean/dirty price calculations for securities.

- Foreign Currency Valuation: Posts FX gains or losses for contracts denominated in foreign currencies.

- Rate Valuation for Forward Contracts: Values forward points and forward premiums/discounts for FX forwards.

- Impairment Procedures: Posts impairment losses and reversals based on valuation rules and credit assessments.

- Current Portion Reclassification: Moves the principal amount of long‑term loans from non‑current to current liabilities in the Balance Sheet.

- Interest Accruals: Posts periodic accruals based on interest amounts for the relevant period.

These are the most commonly used processes, but SAP TRM supports many additional valuation and posting functions depending on organizational policies and the financial instruments in use

 

4.    Link Contracts – References

Sometimes it is necessary to link two separate contracts for reporting or other business requirements. In such cases, the Create Reference (TBRL) option can be used. For example, if a loan needs to be linked with a guarantee, you can create a reference between the two contracts. This linkage can then be utilized for reporting, audit tracking, and overall transparency.

 

5.    Copy Contract

Copy Contract is an easy and efficient way to create a new contract when most of the fields are similar to an existing one. You can access this function through the Collective Processing app or the relevant transaction code.

Simply select the source transaction and choose the Copy option, as shown in the screenshot. The system will create a duplicate of the contract, and you can adjust any fields as needed before saving it

 

 

6.    Reversal

Reversing a contract, activity, or flows can sometimes be a complex process because each contract may have multiple activities and flows posted at different points in time. This often means that multiple reversals must be performed in the correct sequence.

For example, if a reference interest rate has been assigned and an interest flow has already been posted, the system will not allow you to directly reverse the activity. You must follow the same logical order in reverse that you used during the original processing: first reverse the financial posting and the flow, then reverse the assignment of the reference rate, and only after that can you reverse the activity or the entire transaction.

So be careful—if the sequence is not respected, inconsistencies may occur. There are detailed blogs and documentation available on this topic, so it is strongly recommended to understand the end‑to‑end process before performing reversals.

 

7.    Attach Document

The document attachment feature is available for every contract, whether during settlement or while making changes. You can use it to upload supporting documents such as PDFs, Word files, and other relevant materials. When working with banks, there are always important documents that should be attached for future reference and audit purposes.

In the Trade Finance area, a dedicated section is provided specifically for process‑related attachments. This ensures that all documentation linked to letters of credit, guarantees, collections, and other trade finance processes is stored in a structured and easily accessible manner.

 

 

8.    Market Data Provider

We can bring market data into SAP TRM—such as interest rates, forex rates, yield curves, and more. However, this data is not free and is not included with the SAP TRM module. A subscription from a market data provider like Refinitiv, Bloomberg, or others is required.

Your organization needs a corporate license, which can then be integrated with the ERP system. The provider will guide you through the setup once you contact them. After the subscription is active, the integration allows market data to flow automatically into SAP TRM

 

9.    Before Integration with Bank/Financial institute

In today’s environment, Corporate Treasury functions are becoming increasingly demanding, and organizations expect a higher level of automation across all processes. Treasury teams work with multiple external institutions, and these institutions often expect system‑to‑system automation so tasks can be executed without manual intervention. In many cases, they do not want to enter the same information in both SAP and the external institution’s system — which is a completely valid expectation.

SAP is fully capable of integrating with external systems, but before committing to any integration, two key points must be verified: The external institution must actually offer integration services (e.g., data exchange, automated processing, connectivity). The integration method must be clearly defined, such as API, file-based interface, host-to-host, or any other supported mechanism.

If both conditions are met, then integration is technically feasible.

Payments and market data integrations are already common, and many organizations are adopting standardized solutions. However, beyond these typical scenarios, there are other possibilities. For example, SWIFT provides multiple integration options between corporates and banks, even when a bank does not offer a direct API.

Therefore, whenever you plan to work on any integration, ensure that: The external party agrees to support the integration. The integration method is clearly defined and technically supported by both sides.

10.                   Integration Inbound/Outbound

SAP TRM provides extensive integration capabilities both within the organization and with external financial institutions. One of the most common integrations is for market data, where organizations subscribe to a market data provider. Through SAP BTP, this data is automatically transferred into the relevant S/4HANA tables, ensuring accurate and up‑to‑date pricing, yield curves, FX rates, and other valuation inputs.

For payment processing and message exchange, the Multi‑Bank Connectivity (MBC) framework is typically used. MBC enables secure, automated communication between SAP and connected banks.

SAP TRM can also integrate directly with SWIFT, allowing the exchange of financial messages between the corporate system and banks. This requires defining the appropriate inbound and outbound structures to support message formats such as MT and MX.

Similarly, information can be exchanged between S/4HANA and TRM in both directions, depending on the business requirement. The integration possibilities are highly flexible, and SAP places no inherent limitation on how data can flow across systems, provided the structures and interfaces are properly defined

11.                   APIs for Treasury and Risk Management

APIs are available in SAP for retrieving the financial transaction net value, treasury posting journal line items, and position flows. These APIs enable seamless integration with external systems and support automated reporting, reconciliation, and analytics. Further details can be accessed from the link provided below.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/d9c0dd5023e34f0d85ed30fc859496aa/325f953a5fb54319921538882b015b5a.html?locale=en-US

 

12.                   Risk Free Rate - SOFR / €STR / SONIA

With the move from old benchmark rates like LIBOR to new Risk‑Free Rates such as SOFR, interest benchmarks have changed for both new and existing loans. In SAP TRM, the solution is now mature and easy to use.

You create the deal using the new RFR interest condition. Only a small configuration change is needed, and then you can select SOFR or any other RFR directly. Old loans can be shifted from LIBOR to SOFR by updating the interest condition in the same deal. SAP handles the transition smoothly without recreating the loan. This works for both Money Market and Securities products. Blogs are available for step by step guide.

 

13.                   Display Change Document

Changes made to a contract can be monitored using the Display Change Document functionality. This feature is essential for internal analysis and audit requirements. SAP records every modification, including the old value, the new value, and the user who performed the change. This ensures full transparency and traceability throughout the contract lifecycle.

 

 

 

 

 

14.                   FTR_TRD_ADJUST Contract Correction

Sometimes flows are not generated in all relevant tables, which makes it impossible to reverse the contract or remove the flows. In such cases, FTR_TRD_ADJUST can be used to correct inconsistencies between the tables. This issue is rare, but the transaction is very useful when a correction is needed.

 

15.                   IFRS18 – Starting 2027

IFRS 18 is a new reporting standard issued by the IASB that will replace IAS 1 – Presentation of Financial Statements starting January 1, 2027. It introduces a more structured and consistent approach to presenting financial performance, with the goal of enhancing comparability, transparency, and usefulness of financial statements across entities and industries.

Entities must present income and expenses using defined, standardized categories, reducing diversity in practice and making performance reporting more comparable.

Below Link is further help you to implement in SAP

3670330 - Financial Reporting according to IFRS 18 in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP S/4HANA - SAP for Me

https://community.sap.com/t5/financial-management-blog-posts-by-sap/ifrs-18-compliance-for-treasury-and-risk-management-in-sap-cloud-erp-key/ba-p/14299973

 

 

16.                   Collective processing apps

Collective Processing apps provide a far more efficient way to handle activities within specific treasury areas such as Money Market or Trade Finance. They not only allow users to execute multiple processing options from a single place, but they also function like reports, giving visibility into all relevant transactions before taking action.

In my experience, these apps are highly recommended compared to using individual transactions like FTR_CREATE or FTR_EDIT. They offer greater flexibility, a clearer overview of available functions, and a more intuitive way to manage large volumes of deals or operational tasks.

 

 

 

17.                   Additional Account Assignment e.g Cost centre/Profit Centre

Additional account assignments such as Cost Center or Profit Center must be maintained directly in the TRM configuration under the Accounting section. This is done through “Allocate Additional Account Assignment to Account Assignment Reference” or “Additional Account Assignment”, where you also define the appropriate derivation rules. These settings ensure that the correct account assignment objects are automatically derived during contract creation and later during financial postings.

It is important not to assign these objects through OKB9 or by setting a default Profit Center, as this can cause inconsistencies and conflicts with other modules. This will be applied during financial postings such as TBB1.

 

18.                   Additional Fields/Tab in Financial instrument (Contract)

You can add up to two additional tabs in the financial transaction screen, and each tab can contain multiple fields. The design depends entirely on the business requirement—whether you need simple input fields, a table where users can add multiple rows, or fields that integrate with existing calculations or missing processes.

I’ve used this enhancement in many projects, and it’s extremely useful. However, it’s important to understand its limitations. The additional tabs do appear for every financial instrument. Since SAP only allows a maximum of two custom tabs, they must be designed carefully. If the requirement goes beyond what two tabs can handle, it’s better to introduce segments to structure the information more efficiently

BADI:  FTR_CUSTOMER_EXTENT

SPRO>FSCM>TRM>Transaction Manager>Transaction Management>Enhancement

 

19.                   Calander or Date Check

Before concluding a financial transaction, SAP Treasury and Risk Management provides a Date Check function to ensure that key dates—such as due dates, fixing dates, or settlement dates—fall on valid working days. This validation is performed against the working calendars assigned to the company code or currency.

If the entered or system‑calculated date is not a working day (for example, a weekend or a public holiday), the system automatically displays the previous and next available working days. This helps users select an appropriate date and prevents downstream settlement or processing issues.

During transaction creation or settlement, if a date does not comply with any of these calendars, SAP triggers a popup warning, prompting the user to adjust the date accordingly.

This functionality ensures operational accuracy, reduces settlement failures, and aligns all transaction dates with market and counterparty conventions.

 

20.                   Tables in SAP TRM

Finding the correct table in SAP Treasury & Risk Management (TRM) can be confusing because financial transactions span multiple layers: header, activity, flows, conditions, and accounting. SAP provides a very efficient way to discover exactly where data is stored.

FTR_SHOW is the most reliable tool to identify all tables linked to a specific financial transaction. Open FTR_SHOW, Enter Company Code, Enter the Transaction Number, Execute You will see: All tables used for that transaction

If you need to explore beyond the basics, SAP naming conventions help a lot. Useful patterns VTB* → Treasury transaction tables, VTI* → Market data / valuation tables, TLCT* → Condition and lifecycle-related tables. These prefixes quickly guide you to the right technical area.

When you know a field but not the table: Go to SE11 Enter the View (e.g., V_TVBFHA, V_TVBFHAPO), Select the field, Use Where-Used List. This is extremely useful when debugging, enhancing, or writing custom reports.

21.                   CDS view in SAP TRM

CDS query views can be consumed by any reporting tool that supports CDS-based analytics, including SAP Analysis for Microsoft Office, Design Studio, SAP Lumira, and SAP Analytics Cloud. These views provide cleaner, business‑ready data compared to raw tables, making them ideal for developing analytical reports and dashboards. The link below provides the complete list of CDS views available in the TRM module.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/848f8ce21bcd4f67bce77494799e2257/f71f1f5c39374159bb9f07cbc0e05aa2.html?locale=en-US

 

22.                   Financial instrument vs Process

Understanding which processes (Change, Settle, Rollover, etc.) apply to which financial instrument is essential for correct transaction handling in SAP Treasury and Risk Management.

SAP provides a simple way to check which processes are valid for a specific financial instrument using FTR_EDIT. On the right-hand side, select the Financial Instrument (e.g., Money Market, FX, Securities, Derivatives). Observe the left-hand side buttons (Change, Settle, Rollover, Reverse, etc.).

Pressable (active) buttons → These processes are allowed for the selected financial instrument.

Greyed-out (inactive) buttons → These processes are not applicable for that instrument.

Each financial instrument type has its own lifecycle and permitted actions.

 

 

23.                   Product Type Configuration

All product types come preconfigured in the system, and based on business requirements, we can adjust the configuration or create new product types as needed. A product type includes several dependent configuration elements such as Transaction Types, Flow Types, Conditions, and other related settings.

When defining a new product type, all these components must be configured in the correct sequence to ensure consistency and proper system behaviour. One of the easiest and most reliable approaches is to use the Copy tool. It automatically copies all dependent configuration objects along with the product type, which significantly simplifies the setup process and reduces the risk of missing any required configuration.

If you want, I can also expand this into a step‑by‑step guide or a configuration checklist.SPRO>FSCM>TRM>Transaction management>General Settings>Tools>Copy or rename a product type and dependent settings

 

24.                   TBB1 vs TBB1_LC

TBB1 is used to post financial documents for active treasury flows. During migration, however, TBB1_LC is used to post legacy flows. This marks the flows as posted in Treasury without generating any FI documents. For example, if a loan was taken two years ago with a five‑year tenor and we are now moving to TRM, the full amortization schedule will be created in the system. The historical two years of flows will be posted using TBB1_LC to avoid duplicate accounting, while all future flows will be posted through TBB1.

25.                   Financial Document Enhancement

It is possible to capture the required information in financial document fields such as Assignment, Header Text, or Reference. These fields are later utilized by the Finance department for reporting and bank‑clearing activities. This approach also enables updating/replacing the input across multiple fields as needed.

SPRO>FSCM>TRM>Transaction manager> Accounting>Link to other Accounting components> Enhancement in Posting interface

Definition name: TPM_ACCIF_TRAC

26.                   AAR Account Assignment Reference

AAR is used to derive the GL account during financial document posting, and it is assigned to each position at the time of deal creation. Its value is determined through the derivation logic maintained in the Accounting configuration. Although it is technically possible to manually change or correct this field during deal creation or editing, doing so is not recommended. If an adjustment is required, the AAR can be replaced using transaction TPM28.

 

27.                   Hedge management Net Open Exposure vs Balance Sheet Hedging

Net Open Exposure relies on TRM Exposure Management and Cash Management as its primary sources of exposure data. In contrast, Balance Sheet Hedging derives its exposure from FI Balances, FI Open Items, and Cash Management.

28.                   Roles in Treasury and Risk Management

Below are the standard SAP Fiori roles. Assign these roles to the user, and the corresponding Fiori apps will become visible in their launchpad.

SAP_BR_TREASURY_RISK_MANAGER: Leads the treasury department. Evaluates financial statuses and performs overall reporting. Manages traders and their authorizations.

SAP_BR_TREASURY_SPECIALIST_FOE: Concludes and manages financial transactions to hedge foreign exchange and interest rate risks.

SAP_BR_TREASURY_SPECIALIST_MOE: Monitors financial risks, such as credit risks and market risks. Is responsible for mitigation of risks in compliance with the corporate treasury guidelines (hedging policy) and ensures conformity with accounting regulations.

SAP_BR_TREASURY_SPECIALIST_BOE: Processes financial transactions, manages correspondence and sends confirmations to counterparties, imports market data, and calculates net present values.

SAP_BR_TREASURY_ACCOUNTANT: Processes and valuates the treasury positions and makes the relevant postings in Financial Accounting to ensure the accurate portrayal of treasury activities.

29.                   Document Management System for TRM

DMS must be configured correctly to enable the generation of correspondence messages. SAP Note 1320374 provides the detailed steps required for this configuration

30.                   Treasury Analytics

Analytics is a critical requirement for our system landscape, and one of the most valuable components is the Treasury Executive Dashboard delivered as example content within SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC). This dashboard leverages the standard CDS query views available for Treasury Management in the back-end system, providing real‑time insights and actionable intelligence.

Treasury Alerts play a key role in ensuring timely execution of process steps. These alerts support various activities such as correspondence, settlement, release, interest rate updates, postings, and payments—helping teams stay compliant and responsive.

The Debt and Maturity Analysis and Debt and Maturity Profile reports offer detailed visibility into debt positions over time, along with powerful drill‑down capabilities for deeper analysis.

The Market Data Overview provides a consolidated view of financial risks related to foreign exchange (FX) and market data. Users can monitor current and historical FX spot rates, FX swap rates, reference interest rates, security prices, and other key indicators for a selected date.

The Foreign Exchange Overview further enhances risk transparency by summarizing FX‑related exposures, including FX instruments, financial status, cash position, and liquidity forecasts.

Beyond these, a wide range of additional Fiori‑based Treasury reports are available. With the flexibility of CDS views, organizations can also design and build their own SAC stories tailored to specific Treasury requirements

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog.
If you notice anything that needs correction, please feel free to let me know.


Also, tell me if there’s any topic you’d like me to add, or if you’d prefer a step‑by‑step explanation of any section.


If you found this helpful, please share it so others can benefit as well

entry.title: Re: The user should only be able to view a preview of the...
entry.content: In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition, the standard approval UIs for Supplier Invoices do not provide an inline preview for file attachments. entry.title: Partial Goods Receipt API
entry.content:
Dear, for our customer, during inbound processing in WMS, we need a partial goods receipt API to improve our process.

However, aftering testing different APIs we could not find any API.

Can you please provide us with the correct API to use when we want to post a partial goods receipt linked to an inbound delivery item.

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Partial Goods Receipt API
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community, 

There is no dedicated API in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition to post a partial goods receipt directly against an inbound delivery item in WMS/EWM.
Partial GR is not controlled by a specific “partial GR API”, but by how the inbound delivery and warehouse tasks are processed.

Partial GR is only possible using HUs. Here e.g. the HU API and method PostGoodsReceipt can be used.

Try Out | Handling Unit | SAP Business Accelerator Hub 

Partial Goods Receipt for Handling Units | SAP Help Portal

Post Goods Receipt | SAP Help Portal

If a dedicated API to post partial goods receipt is required for your business, I suggest you create a feature request to SAP (##2963059 - How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4 HANA Cloud Product). With an Influence request opened, the feature request gains visibility by other customers and might speed up the release of this functionality in further product versions. 

Thank you and best regards,

SAP Support.

entry.title: S/4HANA Public Cloud: How to restrict Credit Release validity
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I am currently configuring Basic Credit Management (Scope Item BD6) in an S/4HANA Public Cloud environment.

I have a specific requirement regarding the validity of a released document (Documented Credit Decision - DCD). I want to ensure that a manual release granted by a credit manager is only valid for the same day. If the document is not processed (e.g., delivered) on the same day, it should be blocked again and require a new credit check/decision the following day.

For testing purposes, I approved a documented credit decision and scheduled a re-check run. Even after two days, the job skips the document for the re-check and the job log displays the following message: " Document has not been checked because the release date is still valid." 

How can this be maintained correctly?

Thanks in advance!

Best regards
Katja

entry.title: SAP Business AI & Sustainability: HOW
entry.content:
Sustainability is no longer optional—it’s a strategic imperative. Businesses must integrate sustainability into their core operations to meet regulatory demands, investor expectations, and societal responsibilities. SAP Business AI provides powerful tools to automate processes, enhance decision-making, and accelerate sustainability initiatives.

  1. Embedded AI for Seamless Sustainability Integration SAP’s embedded AI capabilities streamline sustainability management by automating ESG reporting, carbon accounting, and compliance tracking. This reduces manual effort while ensuring accuracy and regulatory alignment.

  2. Custom AI Extensions for Tailored Solutions Developers can build custom AI applications on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) to address unique sustainability challenges, ensuring flexibility and scalability.

  3. Advanced AI Techniques for Smarter Decisions

    • Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG): Combines large language models with business data to generate context-aware insights.
    • Vector Embeddings: Converts complex data (documents, images) into analyzable vectors for precise sustainability analytics.

  4. AI Use Cases Across SAP Sustainability Solutions

    • ESG Reporting: Automate report generation in SAP Sustainability Control Tower.
    • Carbon Accounting: Analyze emissions data in SAP Green Ledger with AI-driven insights.
    • Supplier Compliance: Automate compliance checks in SAP S/4HANA for Product Compliance.

  5. Executive Benefits by Role

    • CFOs: Unify financial and ESG data for better reporting and risk management.
    • COOs: Optimize operations with AI-driven risk prediction and automation.
    • CSOs: Embed sustainability across the business with AI-powered tracking and reporting.

SAP Business AI empowers businesses to achieve sustainability goals efficiently. By leveraging embedded AI and custom solutions, companies can reduce costs, improve compliance, and drive meaningful environmental and social impact.

entry.title: Re: Hybrid Integration Pattern- SAP S/4HANA Vendor Data to Azure Service Bus Using SAP Integration S
entry.content: @MateuszPiotrowski  Thanks for the suggestion! Using SAP Event Mesh for native event publishing is definitely a strong event-driven pattern and works very well for decoupling producers and consumers. In our case, Azure Service Bus was chosen to align with the customer’s Azure-first integration strategy and existing messaging landscape. Both approaches are valid, and the right choice depends on enterprise architecture and platform preferences.

entry.title: FI-AA: Possible Issues Post S/4HANA Transition
entry.content:
This blog is to share a few things that were experienced in a S/4HANA - post Project transition and the way it was resolved, successfully. Might be helpful for someone encountering a similar issue.

Fixed Assets:

Once the S/4HANA migration is fully completed say from ECC and later when a new Company Code is directly created in S/4H environ, the Asset module requires some adjustments.

Assuming there is a Chart of Depreciation already existing for a specific Country / Company Code and the new Company Code also requires to be assigned to the existing COD, system does not allow the same. Despite the fact that multiple Company Codes can be assigned to a single Chart of Depreciation, as per standard model.

We might face errors in multiple transaction codes like 'FINSC_LEDGER', 'FAA_CMP' etc. (owing to currency related settings).


Therefore, to overcome this scenario, SAP recommends creating a new Chart of Depreciation and assign the new Comp Code to the same. In addition, a few more settings are also required, as given below.

Check and update transaction Codes 'OADB','OADC' and under SPRO: Asset Accounting > General Valuation > Currencies > Specify the Use of Parallel Currencies

The Screen Prints are just for representation purposes only 😊.

In this regard, please find a link to SAP OSS Note - https://me.sap.com/notes/2882574/E (2882574 - Message ACC_AA116 for a new company code created in SAP S/4HANA)

The note clearly states "currently, there is no other solution available in FI-AA".

Balance C/F - FAGL-GVTR: 

Another error could be encountered during Balance carry forward. This could be due to a missed sequence in close process, by a user. Please find link to SAP OSS Note - https://me.sap.com/notes/3630930 (3630930 - Error in transaction FAGLGVTR although the fiscal year was successful carried forwarded in asset accounting) The note describes further:

Quote: " error message AA594 is issued for each processed asset in the balance carryforward (Errors occurred for asset 1780 60603514-0 in fiscal year 2026). This error message is the information that postprocessing activities (-> AFAR) are necessary.

Nevertheless, at the end, you receive the information:
CCode XXXX Fiscal year change 2026 performed, postprocessing necessary." Unquote.

The note is pretty useful and guides well.

Hope you find the information useful. Thanks for your comments and feedback.

 

Regards,
J. Swaminathan
